coredata.dc:title: Engineering biocompatible carbon dots nano-enzymes hydrogel for efficient antioxidative and anti-inflammatory treatment of dry eye disease

coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Hailing
Yu, Xinxi
Huang, Yin
Yu, Ting
Lan, Huimin
Zhang, Qianqian
Huang, Yongquan
Peng, Xin
Jiang, Zebo
coredata.dc:description: Dry eye disease (DED) is a complex and multifactorial ocular surface disease. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are of pivotal importance in the inflammatory processes and biological dysfunction associated with DED. In this study, an injectable hydrogel, designated as OHACDgel, was created by combining oxidized HA-containing aldehyde groups (OHA) and gelation (gel) via dynamic covalent linkages of the hydrazine bonds, is employed as the carrier, while polyethylene imine-functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CD) can form dynamic chemical bonds with the hydrogel, thus prolonging the retention time of the ocular surface. OHACDgel has been demonstrated to diminish ROS overproduction markedly, reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory factors, inhibit the transformation of macrophages into a pro-inflammatory phenotype, reverse corneal epithelial defects, restore goblet cell function, and enhance tear secretion. Furthermore, the biocompatibility of OHACDgel has been demonstrated, presenting a rapid and straightforward therapeutic option for potential applications in DED.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dry eye disease
Carbon dots
Nano-enzyme
Injectable hydrogel
Antioxidant

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro anti-inflammatory activities of OHACDgel
In vivo treatment effect of DED model
The synthesis of OHAgel and OHACDgel
The retention time on the ocular surface

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Zhuhai Science and Technology Innovation Bureau (No.2420004000007).

This work was supported by the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation of China ( 2023A1515010620 , and 2024A1515012478 ). This work was also funded by the Zhuhai Science and Technology Bureau (No. 2420004000007 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of nanozymes hydrogel for dry eye disease therapy (Created with BioRender.com).

Preparation and Characterization of OHACDgel. (A) The schematic diagram of nanozymes hydrogel. (B) The HRTEM images of PEI-CD. The scale bar is 20 nm. The inset is HRTEM image of PEI-CD. The scale bar is 1 nm. (C) The Raman spectrum of PEI-CD. (D) The FTIR spectrum of PEI-CD. (E) The Zeta potential of CD and PEI-CD. (F) The UV–Vis spectrum of PEI-CD. (G) 1H NMR spectra of HA and OHA, showing the existence of aldehyde groups in modified HA. (H) The gelation of OHA hydrogel. (I) FTIR spectrometry analysis of OHA hydrogel. (J) Frequency-dependent rheology of the OHAgel. (K) Evolution of storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″) during the shear strain increase showing the gel-to-liquid transition at ≈60 % of shear strain.

Biocompatibility of OHACDgel. (A-B) Cell viability of HCEC cells treated with different concentrations of PEI-CD, OHAgel, or OHACDgel leachate for 24 and 48 h. (CD) Cell viability of J774A.1 cells treated with different concentrations of PEI-CD, OHAgel, or OHACDgel leachate for 24 and 48 h. (E) The Calcien-AM/PI staining of HCEC and J774A.1 cells treated by PBS, PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel. (F) The retention time of OHACDgel on the ocular surface was analyzed. (G) The hemolysis of OHACDgel. (H) The wound healing of HCEC cells under different treatments. The scale bar is 400 μm.

In vitro antioxidant of OHACDgel. (A) Fluorescence images showing reduction of intracellular ROS staining by DCFH-DA probe in HCEC cells after treated with PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel under hypertonic conditions. The scale bar is 100 μm. (B) Fluorescence images showing reduction of intracellular ROS staining by DCFH-DA probe in LPS-induced J774A.1 cells. The scale bar is 100 μm. (C) The flow cytometry shows reduction of intracellular ROS in HCEC cells. (D) The flow cytometry showing reduction of intracellular ROS in LPS-induced J774A.1 cells. (E) Volcano plots of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) after being treated with OHACDgel treatment. (F-G) Functional analyses from DEAS events in HCEC cells after OHACDgel treatment, including GO and KEGG. (H) Heat maps of significantly down-regulated or down-regulated DEGs based on the expressed genes associated with oxidative stress in the HCECs after OHACDgel treatment (|Fold Change| ≥ 2 and P < 0.05). (I) The Venn diagram illustrates the genes at the response to oxidative stress reactive oxygen species, cellular responses to oxidative stress, and cellular responses to reactive oxygen species. (J) Heat maps of significantly down-regulated or down-regulated DEGs based on Venn diagram.

In vitro anti-inflammatory activities of OHACDgel. (A) Functional analyses from OHACDgel treatment DEAS events in HCEC cells under hypertonic conditions, including GO and KEGG. (B) The Venn diagram illustrates the genes at the intersection of regulation of inflammatory response, positive regulation of cytokine production, positive regulation of immune response, innate immune response, cytokine signaling in the immune system, and cellular response to cytokine stimulus. (C) KEGG pathways analysis of DEGs between the control group and OHACDgel-treated group. (D) GSEA demonstrates the enrichment of gene sets related to the TNF-α/NF-κb signaling pathway. (E) Heat maps of significantly down-regulated or down-regulated DEGs based on the expressed genes associated with inflammation in the HCECs after OHACDgel treatment (|Fold Change| ≥ 2 and P < 0.05). (F-G) Representative flow cytometric graphs and corresponding statistical analysis showing the expression of CD86 and CD206, as detected by flow cytometry. (H) Expression levels of IL-6 and IL-1β were measured using an ELISA assay.

In vivo therapeutic effect of OHACDgel against DED. (A) Schematic of the in vivo mouse DED model establishment, treatment, and subsequent assessment. (B, C, D) The corneal fluorescence staining, Schirmer test, and body weight during the established DED model. (E) Observation of the mouse cornea through a slit lamp. (F) Representative images of corneal fluorescein staining and (G) statistical analysis of the fluorescein score. (H) Thread length in a 20 s Schirmer test of different groups in consecutive days and (I) paired comparison of body weights. (J) H&E and PAS staining showing the conjunctiva structure and goblet cell numbers. Scale bar = 100 μm.

head.ce:title: Engineering biocompatible carbon dots nano-enzymes hydrogel for efficient antioxidative and anti-inflammatory treatment of dry eye disease

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Research Center of Molecular Imaging, Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao University Joint Laboratory of Interventional Medicine, The Fifth Affiliated Hospital

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dry eye disease (DED) is a complex and multifactorial ocular surface disease. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are of pivotal importance in the inflammatory processes and biological dysfunction associated with DED. In this study, an injectable hydrogel, designated as OHACDgel, was created by combining oxidized HA-containing aldehyde groups (OHA) and gelation (gel) via dynamic covalent linkages of the hydrazine bonds, is employed as the carrier, while polyethylene imine-functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CD) can form dynamic chemical bonds with the hydrogel, thus prolonging the retention time of the ocular surface. OHACDgel has been demonstrated to diminish ROS overproduction markedly, reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory factors, inhibit the transformation of macrophages into a pro-inflammatory phenotype, reverse corneal epithelial defects, restore goblet cell function, and enhance tear secretion. Furthermore, the biocompatibility of OHACDgel has been demonstrated, presenting a rapid and straightforward therapeutic option for potential applications in DED.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dry eye disease (DED) is a common ocular condition that leads to discomfort and impaired vision, significantly affecting an individual’s quality of life [1]. The widespread use of electronic devices and sedentary lifestyles have been implicated in the rising incidence of DED [2]. Prolonged exposure to intense ultraviolet radiation, high levels of ionizing radiation, air pollution, and excessive screen time can damage the cornea, located at the front of the eye [3]. This exposure induces oxidative stress, causing harm to the ocular surface and contributing to DED through the promotion of inflammation and increased osmotic pressure in the tear film. Inflammation, driven by innate and adaptive immune responses, perpetuates the cycle of DED [4]. Alongside inflammation, reactive oxygen species (ROS) play a crucial role in regulating the inflammatory processes within this cycle [5,6]. The excessive accumulation of ROS in ocular tissues, which are inadequately neutralized, activates inflammatory pathways, leading to the loss of goblet cells, decreased corneal wettability, abnormalities in corneal epithelial cells, and impaired immune response [7]. The over-expression of ROS can stimulate chronic oxidative stress, adversely affecting tissue integrity oxidative. Consequently, developing ROS scavengers has become a pivotal strategy for preventing and treating ROS-related chronic diseases [8].

Nano-enzymes, a novel class of biomaterials, have garnered significant interest for their potential in ROS scavenging to aid wound healing and reduce inflammation [9]. These nano-enzymes exhibit a multitude of enzymatic activities, such as catalase (CAT), peroxidase (POD), superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like properties effectively modulating the redox environment [10–12]. Carbon dots (CD) have also generated interest due to their ease of surface functionalization, making them valuable in biomedical and environmental applications [13]. In addition to their distinctive optical characteristics, CDs exhibit low cytotoxicity and favorable biocompatibility, making them a promising alternative to traditional quantum dots in a range of biological applications, including cell labeling, disease diagnosis, bioimaging, and drug release [14–16]. Since their discovery, researchers have continued to refine and innovate based on the catalytic activity of CD [15,17]. CD is composed of carbon as the primary structural unit and has been demonstrated to exhibit excellent ROS scavenging activity [18].

In recent years, treatments for DED primarily focus on symptom relief, reducing ocular inflammation, and improving tear film stability [19]. However, these medications can only provide temporary relief by wetting and lubricating the ocular surface, rather than providing a fundamental resolution to the underlying issue of DED [20]. Many pharmaceutical agents are challenging to solubilize in eye drops and are quickly washed away by tears, limiting their therapeutic effectiveness [21]. Frequent administration (four to five times daily) is often necessary to achieve adequate dosing, increasing the risk of adverse effects [22]. It is therefore imperative to develop a novel drug delivery system with the objective of prolonging the retention time of drugs or nanomaterials on the ocular surface, with the aim of providing more effective relief from DED [23–25]. Hydrogels represent a leading option for a drug delivery system due to a number of advantageous characteristics, including their excellent hydrophilicity, transparency, biocompatibility, and porous three-dimensional structure that supports cellular activity and metabolite transport [26–28].

In this study, a novel nano-enzyme hydrogel was developed by incorporating nano-enzymes into an oxidized hyaluronic acid (HA)-based hydrogel. The injectable hydrogel, designated as OHACDgel, was created by comminating oxidized HA-containing aldehyde groups (OHA), PEI-capped carbon dots (PEI-CD), and gelatin (gel) via dynamic covalent linkages of the hydrazine bonds. The combination of these elements establishes a network within the hydrogel, with aldehyde-amino crosslinking facilitating enhanced ocular retention. The OHACDgel has demonstrated antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, potentially beneficial dry eye treatment. Additionally, OHACDgel interacts with various cell types within the corneal microenvironment, influencing the polarization of pro-inflammatory macrophages and facilitating corneal epithelial cell regeneration (Fig. 1 ). The nano-enzyme hydrogel can scavenge excess ROS, reduce oxidative damage, inhibit the expression of inflammatory factors, and concurrently prolong the retention time on the ocular surface.

The synthesis of the PEI-CD is illustrated in Fig. 2A. The PEI-CD was initially prepared by the previously reported method [29,30]. The synthesis of PEI-capped CD was conducted via the one-way microwave method, employing a high power to facilitate the process. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images revealed that the PEI-CD possessed uniformly spherical structures with a diameter of approximately 4 nm. The interplanar spacing of PEI-CD was approximately 0.32 nm, as shown in Fig. 2B. The Raman spectrum of PEI-CD exhibited the characteristic peaks of CD, including the G and D peaks (Fig. 2C). The absorption peak observed at 3230 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of PEI-CD can be attributed to the overlap of hydroxyl (OH) and amino (NH) vibrations. The peak at 3603 cm−1 is attributed to the hydroxyl (OH) vibrational mode, while the peak at 1623 cm−1 is primarily associated with the telescopic vibrational peak of hydroxyl (C=O) in acyl groups (Fig. 2D). Furthermore, the PEI-capped CD exhibited a positive zeta potential of 10 mV, which was attributed to the exposure of the amino group on the PEI surface (Fig. 2E). The positively charged CD can induce the transient opening of corneal epithelial cell tight junctions, thereby facilitating cellular uptake and enabling greater penetration into the corneal stroma than is possible with conventional antimicrobials [31]. Furthermore, they exhibit a prolonged residence time within the corneal tissue [32]. Fig. 2F illustrates the UV–Vis spectrum of PEI-CD, which did not exhibit any absorption peaks within the 600–900 nm range.

The injectable properties of the fabricated OHA hydrogel were demonstrated to be a consequence of the dynamic covalent linkages of the hydrazine bond. The formation of hydrogels through the hydrazine bond is typically homogeneous and exhibits good mechanical properties. The initial step involved the synthesis of oxidized HA-containing aldehyde groups (OHA) through the oxidation of sodium periodates (NaIO₄). The purified products of OHA, OHAgel, and gelatin were confirmed using 1H nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H NMR) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). Fig. 2G illustrates the 1H NMR profiles, which provide insight into the chemical structure of HA and OHA. As evidenced by the NMR spectrum, the presence of new peaks corresponding to the aldehyde groups in OHA is indicated by the black square. The FTIR spectrum of OHA in Fig. 2I exhibited a distinctive peak associated with the aldehyde group at 1730 cm−1, which is indicative of the presence of this functional group [33]. The 1H NMR and FTIR spectrums substantiate the successful synthesis of OHA. Furthermore, as demonstrated in Fig. 2I, the peaks within the range of 3000 to 3750 cm−1 and 2930 cm−1 are attributed to the -OH and -CH-stretching vibrations, respectively. The appearance of the CN peak at 1633 cm−1 provides evidence that the success of Schiff's base reaction has been formed between the aldehyde group of OHA and the amino group of gelatin. This observation corroborates the successful preparation of the OHA hydrogel. The mixture of OHA and gelatin could readily undergo gelation, as a result of the successful modification of OHA (Fig. 2H). The efficient aldehyde-amino covalent cross-linking reaction allows for the formation of the hydrogel. In consideration of the overall rotational frequency scale, it was observed that G' consistently exceeded G", indicative of a solid-state configuration of the OHAgel. Following the mixing of the OHA and gelatin solution, the storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G") demonstrated an increase over time, reaching a point of equality at approximately 60 %. This confirms the formation of the gel from the liquid phase (Fig. 2K).

Corneal epithelial cells and macrophages play essential role in the pathophysiology and treatment of DED. The biocompatibility of the OHACDgel is crucial for promoting cell proliferation and enhancing corneal healing [34]. First, to assess its cytotoxicity, both human corneal epithelial cells (HCEC) and mouse macrophages (J774A.1) cells were treated with OHACDgel and analyzed using a cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. Results showed that the OHACDgel groups exhibited higher cell viability compared to the control group, with concentrations up to 100 μg/mL in HCEC cells. Additionally, the cell viabilities of the PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel remained slightly above 90 % at a concentration of 100 μg/mL, indicating that the OHACDgel exhibited negligible cytotoxicity in HCEC and J774A.1 cells (Fig. 3A-D). For live/dead staining, a confocal laser scanning microscope was used to evaluate the biocompatibility of the OHACDgel after treatment with PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel for 24 h. The intense green fluorescence and negligible red fluorescence observed excellent biocompatibility of OHACDgel in HCEC cells and J774A.1 cells (Fig. 3E). The duration of contact between hydrogel and the ocular surface is a critical factor in the efficacy of clinical treatment. The retention time on the ocular surface was evaluated, as illustrated in Fig. 3F. The hydrogels were administered topically to the corneal surface, followed by four manual blinks at one-minute intervals. After 8 blinks in 120 s, the PBS was removed, while the OHAgel was observed to prolong the PEI-CD retention time, indicating that the effect of the nano-enzyme hydrogel prolonged the retention time. The release behavior of OHACDgel was also investigated and demonstrated that the OHACDgel could facilitate the release of PEI-CD over a period of 96 h in Fig. S1.

Furthermore, the hemolysis levels in the groups of PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel were within the normal range and showed no statistical significance (Fig. 3G and S2). The results of the wound healing tests indicated that the hydrogel exerts a beneficial effect on cell proliferation and migration. The cells in the CD-loaded hydrogel groups demonstrated accelerated migration within 24 h. The OHACDgel group demonstrated healing percentages of 40.2 % compared with 27.6 % of the PBS groups (Fig. 3H). Overall, these findings indicate that the OHACDgel exhibits excellent biocompatibility, effectively promotes cellular proliferation, potentially enhances tissue regeneration, and successfully aids in corneal tissue reconstruction.

Notably, corneal epithelial cells are central to the pathological process of DED, as they directly respond to adverse environmental conditions and trigger the innate immune response [35]. The microenvironment of DED was found to exhibit an accumulation of ROS and a state of chronic inflammation [36]. Oxidative stress contributes significantly to the pathogenesis of DED. To ascertain the ROS-scavenging ability of OHACDgel in vitro, ROS production was quantified in HCEC using a 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) probe under hypertonic conditions (HOM, 500 mOsM), which induces ROS overproduction. Hyperosmolarity of the tear film has been demonstrated to induce elevated ROS levels, thereby exacerbating the inflammatory response [37]. Therefore, the effective elimination of excess ROS can assist in the regulation of the inflammatory response and the cessation of the vicious cycle of ocular disease [38]. The high-stress (HOM) groups exhibited the highest green fluorescence, indicating significant ROS accumulation due to the hyperosmolarity medium. The groups treated with PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel exhibited markedly lower green fluorescence compared with the HOM group, indicating that OHACDgel demonstrated an exceptional capacity for scavenging ROS in HCEC cells (Fig. 4A and C). It is noteworthy that the treatment with OHACDgel resulted in the detection of a minimal level of ROS, which was observed to be nearly indistinguishable from that of normal cells.

Macrophages are a key component of the immune regulatory system and play a crucial role in the pathogenesis of DED. Beyond their strong phagocytic and antigen-presenting abilities, macrophages secrete a wide variety of proteins, inflammatory mediators, and chemokines upon activation, thereby exacerbating inflammation and advancing DED progression [39]. It can be posited that both cell types are viable targets for DED therapy. Regulating the inflammatory response in these cells may be an effective strategy to reduce cell death and corneal tissue damage associated with DED. To ascertain the potential therapeutic efficacy of the proposed treatment for DED, an in vitro study was conducted utilizing lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced oxidative stress stimulation and inflammation in J774A.1 macrophages. The data obtained from confocal laser scanning microscopy demonstrated a notable elevation in the intracellular ROS level of macrophages following stimulation with 1 μg/mL LPS for 24 h (Fig. 4B). Of particular interest is the observation that the PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel treatments exhibited a pronounced inhibitory effect on LPS-induced ROS levels. As illustrated in Fig. 4D, there is a notable reduction in DCF fluorescence in the OHACDgel groups relative to the LPS group, indicating the overexpression of ROS in J774A.1 cells induced by LPS can be effectively inhibited by the nano-enzyme hydrogel. The OHACDgel has exhibited a noteworthy capacity for antioxidant activity.

The potential anti-DED mechanism of OHACDgel was further investigated through RNA sequencing to identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs) in HCEC cells following treatment with OHACDgel or PEI-CD under hypertonic conditions. Notably, a total of 5239 DEGs were identified, comprising 2793 up-regulated and 2446 down-regulated genes when compared to the hypertonic control (Fig. 4E). Comparisons analysis with the PEI-CD group under the same hypertonic conditions revealed 6285 DEGs, which included 3264 up-regulated and 3021 down-regulated genes (Fig. S3A). Cross-analysis identified 578 genes that were consistently up-regulated across both treatments and 695 genes that were uniformly down-regulated, as shown in the Venn diagram (Fig. S3B). A heat map of 640 oxidative stress-related genes highlighted 61 genes with significant expression changes following OHACDgel treatment in comparison to both control and PEI-CD groups (Fig. S3D). The Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathways analysis and Gene Ontology (GO) indicated that the DEGs were enriched in response to oxidative stress KEGG pathways, with the response to oxidative stress pathway being the most enriched (71 genes), compared to the hypertonic control (Fig. 4F). Furthermore, GO analysis revealed that the DEGs were enriched in various categories, including biological processes (10 GO terms), cellular components (10 GO terms), and molecular functions (10 GO terms) (Fig. 4G). Within the three process categories, the most represented processes included oxidoreductase activity, response to oxygen levels, stress response, cytokine signaling in the immune system and mitochondrion, respectively. Further analysis of genes associated with the oxidative stress response confirmed that key pathways were altered in the control and PEI-CD groups following OHACDgel treatment (Fig. S3D). Notable pathways included oxidative stress response, autophagy regulation, ATM-dependent DNA damage response, and ferroptosis (Fig. S3G). After OHACDgel treatment, significant changes were observed in genes altered in the control group but not in the PEI-CD group (Fig. S3E), indicating enhanced activity in pathways regulating oxidative stress response, reactive oxygen species response, and cytokine production (Fig. S3H). Importantly, only in the PEI-CD group did OHACDgel treatment cause significant alterations in oxidative stress regulation, cellular responses to lipid stimuli, apoptotic signaling, and phosphorylation (Fig. S3I).

Figs. 4H-4J illustrate that 444 DEGs were enriched in oxidative stress-related pathways following treatment with OHACDgel, compared to the hypertonic control group. 71 genes were implicated in the oxidative stress response. In addition, 34 genes were associated with ROS, 39 genes with cellular responses to oxidative stress, and 22 genes with cellular reactions to ROS. We further assessed the expression levels of several key genes following OHACDgel treatment. Notably, the expressions of HSPA8, MAPK9, ATM, HDAC2, RELA, AIFM1, SOD1, FXN, MDM2, SOD2, and CAT were significantly decreased. Conversely, the expressions of PXN, MAPK3, AKT1, MB, STK25, OGG1, MAP2K4, MAPT, MAPK13, and MAPK8 were significantly increased after treatment (Fig. 4J). Collectively, these findings suggest that OHACDgel effectively inhibits oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-inflammatory activities of OHACDgel
ce:section.ce:para: DED is increasingly recognized as a multifactorial condition, closely linked to inflammatory activities of the ocular surface, triggered by factors such as environmental stress, autoimmune disorders, and extended screen time, which disrupts tear production and stability [19]. Inflammatory mediators, including cytokines and chemokines, further exacerbate symptoms by damaging the ocular surface and altering gland function [40]. This bidirectional relationship amplifies discomfort and visual disturbances in patients, underscoring the need to target inflammation in the management and treatment of DED. Functional and pathway enrichment analyses were performed to investigate further and visually illustrate the interactions among immune-related signaling pathways in HCEC cells post-treatment with OHACDgel under hypertonic conditions (Figs. 5A-C). The analysis identified significant enrichment in terms such as “regulation of inflammatory response”, “positive regulation of cytokine production”, “positive regulation of immune response”, “innate immune response”, “cytokine signaling in the immune system”, and “cellular response to cytokine stimulus”, all of which are associated with inflammation and immune responses. Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) further showed that immune-related gene sets were significantly expressed after OHACDgel treatment under hypertonic conditions, particularly those connected to the TNF-α/NF-κb signaling pathway (Figs. 5D). Specifically, Fig. 5E shows that inflammatory-related genes (e.g., IL33, IL18) and chemotaxis-related genes (e.g., CXCL1, CXCL10, and CXCL8) were downregulated following OHACDgel treatment. These findings suggest that OHACDgel exerts significant effects through extensive modulation of pathways associated with DED threats in vitro. Notably, the expression of TNIP1, IL6ST, IDO1, ATM, STING1, IRF3, TNFSF4, FOXP1, IL33, TNFAIP3, NR1H3, NFKBIZ, CX3CL1, and IL18 was observed to decrease following treatment with OHACDgel, as illustrated in the corresponding Fig. 5E. DED is increasingly recognized as a multifactorial condition, closely linked to inflammatory activities of the ocular surface, triggered by factors such as environmental stress, autoimmune disorders, and extended screen time, which disrupts tear production and stability. Inflammatory mediators, including cytokines and chemokines, exacerbate symptoms by damaging the ocular surface and altering gland function. This bidirectional relationship not only amplifies discomfort and visual disturbances in patients, but also highlights the importance of targeting inflammation in the management and treatment of DED. We also evaluated 236 inflammation-associated genes, depicted in the heat map shown in Fig. S4A. After OHACDgel treatment, the heat map for 97 inflammation-related genes revealed consistent trends compared to both the control and PEI-CD groups (Fig. S3D). These genes primarily participate in the positive regulation of the TNF signaling pathway and cytokine production (Figs. S4B and S4E).

Following OHACDgel treatment, significant changes were evident in the altered genes within the control group, while no such changes were noted in the PEI-CD group (Fig. S3E). The altered genes in the control group were mainly associated with the TLR4 signaling cascade, which indicates activation of the TNF signaling pathway with myd88 independence. Conversely, significant alterations in genes related to the TNF signaling pathway occurred solely in the PEI-CD group after treatment with OHACDgel, as illustrated in the heat maps in Figs. S4D and S4G.

Next, to evaluate the anti-inflammatory effects of OHACDgel, flow cytometry (FCM) was used to measure the expression of CD206 (marker for M2-type anti-inflammatory macrophages) and CD86 (marker for M1-type pro-inflammatory macrophages). After 24 h of stimulation with 1 μg/mL LPS, we observed a marked increase in ROS levels and significant upregulation of CD86, indicating polarization towards M1-type macrophages induced by LPS. In contrast, PEI-CD treatment and OHAgel treatment suppressed CD86 expression while simultaneously enhancing the expression of CD206. Notably, OHACDgel demonstrated an even greater ability to inhibit CD86 and promote CD206 expression (Fig. 5F and G). Furthermore, PEI-CD and OHAgel treatment effectively reduced the LPS-induced increase in IL-6 and IL-1β, with OHACDgel showing stronger inhibition of these inflammatory cytokines (Fig. 5H). These findings demonstrate that the OHACD hydrogel effectively shift macrophages polarization from the pro-inflammatory M1 subtype to the anti-inflammatory M2 subtypes, significantly reducing levels of inflammatory factors IL-6 and IL-1β. These results indicate that OHACD hydrogel can successfully transform macrophages from the proinflammatory M1 subtype to the anti-inflammatory M2 subtype. This shift is associated with a significant anti-inflammatory effect, suggesting that OHACD hydrogel may be an effective therapeutic option for treating DED. This transformation not only reduces inflammation but may also promote healing and protect against further damage in the ocular environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo treatment effect of DED model
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the therapeutic effect of OHACDgel on DED in vivo, we established a mouse DED model. All animal procedures were conducted in accordance with the guidelines set forth by the Animal Ethics Committee of Sun Yat-sen University. The specific protocols for the treatments are illustrated in Fig. 6A. The DED model was initially established using 0.2 % benzalkonium chloride (BAC) twice daily for seven days [41]. BAC is a commonly utilized agent for the induction of DED with acute corneal epithelial damage, excessive ROS accumulation, and corneal inflammation [42]. The successful establishment of the DED model was validated through the Schirmer test and corneal fluorescein staining scores. Compared to the control group, the DED group presented with a decline in Schirmer test scores and an elevation in fluorescein staining (Fig. 6B), along with corneal edema, epithelial defects, and dysfunctional tear secretion. No statistically significant variance was observed in Schirmer test and fluorescein staining score between every individual eye of the two groups of mice (P > 0.05, Fig. 6C), confirming the DED model with consistency was effectively induced. Additionally, no significant weight differences were observed between the groups after seven days of BAC induction (P > 0.05), demonstrating BAC did not give rise to serious systemic side effects (Fig. 6D).

Once the DED model was established, subsequently, the anti-inflammatory and antioxidative effects of OHACDgel in vivo were evaluated. The mice, which exhibited a score of >12, were randomly divided into four groups and treated with PBS, PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel twice daily for 3 days. The efficacy was evaluated through clinical observation, Schirmer testing, and histopathology analysis. The extent of corneal epithelium damage and tear deficiency was monitored at 0-, 1-, 2-, and 3-day interval. DED is clinically characterized by corneal surface damage and tear deficiency, which occur daily. Slit-lamp images indicated that the treatment groups (PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel) exhibited minimal discomfort compared to the control group, thereby verifying the in vivo antioxidative effect of OHACDgel (Fig. 6E-F). Following a three-day treatment period, the corneal defect (green fluorescence staining) persisted in both the DED and PEI-CD groups. Following a 3-day course of treatment, plaque staining was observed in the control groups, whereas the OHACDgel exhibited minimal micropunctate staining. A 0–16 point system was employed to clinically score each eye, and it was found that the OHACDgel could significantly lower the incidence of corneal ulcers in comparison to the PEI-CD or OHAgel groups (Fig. 6G). The Schirmer test was conducted to assess the condition of tear secretion. The measurement of average tear volume is a crucial clinical indicator in the diagnosis of DED symptoms [43]. The data indicated that the treated group exhibited higher tear volumes than the control group, and the OHACDgel group had a high tear volume, which was observed to be nearly indistinguishable from that of normal eyes (Fig. 6H). Additionally, no significant differences in weight changes were observed among PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel (P > 0.05). This suggests that none of these treatments influenced the body weight of the mice (Fig. 6I).

Histomorphology evaluation was conducted to assess the structural and morphological changes in ocular organs following various treatments. The tear film comprises three layers: mucous, water, and lipid, secreted by multiple eye tissues and cells. Damage to these tissues can accelerate tear film decomposition and evaporation, leading to an imbalance in tear film homeostasis. Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining is a histological technique used to detect polysaccharides, including glycoproteins and glycolipids, and is particularly effective for highlighting mucins abundant in goblet cells of the conjunctiva [44]. Using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining and PAS staining, normal corneas exhibit compact and orderly cellular organization without inflammatory cell infiltration. However, in the established DED model, destruction and shedding of the superficial cortical layer of the cornea were observed, along with significant thinning. Epithelial cells appeared wrinkled and irregular, and the corneal interstitial layer exhibited thickening, loosening, increased vacuoles, and infiltration of inflammatory cells–consistent with dry eye syndrome. After treatment with OHACDgel, a greater recovery in corneal cell morphology and structure was observed compared to the PEI-CD and OHAgel groups, which showed limited improvement (Fig. 6J, S5, and S6). Collectively, these findings suggest that OHACDgel may enhance corneal tissue repair and recovery following BAC-induced epithelial injury.

ROS inflicts considerable damage to the ocular surface at various stages of the inflammatory cycle, thereby accelerating the progression of DED. However, the current ocular surface therapies are associated with a slow onset of action and the potential for ocular complications with long-term use. Considering these considerations, we have developed a nano-enzyme hydrogel for the highly effective treatment of DED. This approach is based on the ability of the nano-enzymes to scavenge excess ROS, thereby reducing oxidative damage and inhibiting the expression of inflammatory factors. Concurrently, corneal epithelial cell injury and macrophage stimulation represent pivotal developments in the inflammatory cascade. The OHACDgel was observed to exert a robust inhibitory effect on the DED vicious cycle at its inception, effectively suppressing the excessive oxidative stress generated, as well as the repolarization and macrophage processes, which are integral components of the DED vicious cycle. The OHACDgel includes biocompatible elements with minimal adverse effects, which prolong the retention time on the ocular surface, thereby suggesting potential for clinical applications.

The polyethylenimine, 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), and glycerol were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). The Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was obtained from Dojindo (Japan). The Minimum Essential Medium (MEM), Dulbecco's modified Eagles medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum, penicillin, streptomycin, and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were obtained from Gibco (USA). The Calcien AM/PI kits were obtained from KeyGen BioTECH, a company based in China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of OHAgel and OHACDgel
ce:section.ce:para: The PEI-CD was synthesized via the microwave method. The initial step involved the combination of 25 mL of glycerol with 6 mL of a PBS solution. Subsequently, 0.5 g of polyethylenimine (PEI, 25 kMW) was introduced to the mixture. Subsequently, the mixture was subjected to a 10-min heating process in a micro-oven. Subsequently, the mixture underwent dialysis, resulting in a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da. Subsequently, the mixture was subjected to dialysis for a period of two days, followed by lyophilization, with the objective of obtaining PEI-CD powder.

Hyaluronic acid was dissolved in 150 mL of deionized water. The reaction was allowed to proceed for a period of three hours in the presence of a solution of sodium periodate. The reaction was terminated by the addition of ethylene glycol for a period of one hour. Subsequently, the mixture was subjected to dialysis with a molecular weight cut-off of 3.5 kDa. Subsequently, the mixture was subjected to dialysis for a period of three days, after which it was lyophilized, thereby producing the OHA. The OHA solution (4 wt%) and gelation (Type B, 20 wt%) were combined at room temperature to create the OHAgel. The OHA solution (4 wt%) and gelation (Type B, 20 wt%) were combined at room temperature to create the OHACDgel, which included PEI-CD.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the PEI-CD were obtained using a JEOL JEM-F200 microscope, with accompanying mapping images. The crystal structure of PEI-CD was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD). The zeta potential and particle size were determined using a zetasizer (Zetasizer Nano-ZS, Malvern). The ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Thermal) was employed to capture the ultraviolet-visible absorbance.

J774A.1 cells were seeded in 6-well plates and stimulated with LPS (1 μg/mL) for 24 h to polarize them towards M1 macrophages. Subsequently, the leaching solution of hydrogel was added and incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were collected to analyze the expression of macrophage polarization markers using flow cytometry. Additionally, the effect of OHACDgel intervention on the expression levels of LPS-induced IL-1β and IL-6 was assessed through ELISA.

The HCEC cells were seeded into 24-well plates and stimulated using a high-stress medium, which induced the accumulation of ROS. Subsequently, the leaching solution of hydrogel was added and co-incubated for 24 h. J774A.1 cells were seeded in 6-well plates and stimulated with LPS (1 μg/mL) for 24 h to polarize them towards M1 macrophages. Subsequently, the leaching solution of hydrogel was added and co-incubated for 24 h. Then, the antioxidative capacity of OHACDgel was evaluated through DCFH-DA staining and imaging with a confocal laser scanning microscope. The quantity of ROS was analyzed using flow cytometry, with the resulting data expressed as mean fluorescence intensity.

The biocompatibility of the OHACDgel was evaluated through the assessment of cell viability and the utilization of live/dead cell staining. The viability of cells exposed to OHACDgel and PEI-CD was evaluated through the use of the CCK8 assay. The absorbance at 450 nm was quantified using a microplate reader for the CCK8 assay. The HCEC cells and J774A.1 cells were seeded into 6-well plates and the leaching solution of PEI-CD and OHACDgel were added to the medium, where they were co-incubated for 24 h. The live/dead cell assay was conducted using propidium iodide (PI) and Calcien AM kits, and the results were imaged using a confocal laser scanning microscope.

The human corneal epithelial cells (HCEC) and the murine macrophage cells (J774A.1) were procured from Guangzhou Jennio Biotech Co., Ltd. (China). The HCEC cells were cultured in Minimum Essential Medium (MEM) (Gibco, USA), supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco), 100 U/mL penicillin, and 100 U/mL streptomycin (Solarbio, Beijing, China), at 37 °C in a 5 % CO₂ incubator. The J774A.1 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, Gibco, USA) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco, USA), 100 U/mL penicillin, and 100 U/mL streptomycin (Gibco, USA) at 37 °C in a 5 % CO₂ incubator.

Human corneal epithelial cells (HCECs) were treated with OHACDgel, PEI-CD, or a control solution under hypertonic conditions for 24 h. Following treatment, cells were isolated using trypsin and washed twice with cold PBS. The cell pellet was collected through centrifugation, rapidly frozen in liquid nitrogen, and sent to IGE Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China) for transcriptome sequencing. This process included sample testing, library construction, and sequencing. After quality control of the libraries, sequencing was conducted using Illumina PE150 technology (Illumina, USA).

Differential expression analysis was performed between each treatment group using DESeq2, and volcano plots were generated to visualize differentially expressed genes (DEGs) in response to OHACDgel, PEI-CD, and control treatments. A Venn diagram was created to illustrate the overlap of DEGs among the treatment groups. To identify key pathways influenced by OHACDgel in HCEC cells, a Gene Ontology (GO) enrichment analysis was performed using the goana function within the limma Bioconductor package. This analysis identified the most enriched GO terms across biological processes, cellular components, and molecular functions, applying a false discovery rate (FDR) of 0.05. Functional classification of DEGs was conducted using GO analysis to further elucidate their biological roles. Heat maps were generated to illustrate the significant changes in DEGs related to oxidative stress and inflammatory responses in HCECs after OHACDgel treatment (|Fold Change| ≥ 2 and P < 0.05). Additionally, functional analyses of DEGs associated with oxidative stress and inflammatory pathways were performed using Metascape (https://metascape.org/gp/index.html#/main/step1) [45].

The animal studies were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of the Fifth Affiliated Hospital of Sun Yat-sen University (Approval No. 514). Adult male C57BL/6 mice (6–8 weeks old, weighing 20–25 g) were chosen as experimental animal model and maintained under a 12-h light/dark cycle at room temperature with continuous availability of food and water. A 0.2 % benzalkonium chloride (BAC) solution was administered to construct the DED model in mice. Briefly, 5 μL of 0.2 % BAC eye drops (w/v) were instilled in both eyes of the mice twice daily for seven consecutive days. The control group was treated with 0.9 % sterile saline, while the mice suffering from DED were randomly assigned to four experimental groups (n = 6 per group) and delivered medication via 5 μL topical eye drop solutions (PBS, 0.01 % PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel). These treatments were administered twice daily for 3 days, with daily monitoring of body weight. All procedures were performed according to the prescribed guidelines.

The corneal epithelial damage was assessed using fluorescein staining, where sodium fluorescein was instilled into the conjunctival sac and observed under a slit-lamp microscope equipped with a cobalt blue filter. The cornea was separated into four quadrants (nasal, temporal, superior, and inferior), with each quadrant was graded based on the following criteria indicating corneal epithelial damage: 0 = no staining, 1 = mild punctate staining, 2 = marked punctate or mild coalescent staining, 3 = marked coalescent or mild patchy staining, and 4 = marked patchy staining. Besides, tear secretion was analyzed through quantitative measurement of cotton threads in accordance with the outlined instructions. The cotton threads were inserted on the lower palpebral conjunctiva, one-third from the lateral canthus, and the length of the wetted thread was measured in millimeters at 20 s without anesthesia.

The therapeutic effectiveness was monitored daily for three days, and the mice were euthanized after overdose anesthesia once their DED was cured. The eyes and adnexa collected from the animals were then underwent fixation in paraformaldehyde and FAS eyeball fixative (Servicebio, China) for subsequent paraffin embedding. The tissues were cut into sagittal sections with a thickness of 5 μm and preserved at room temperature. Standard histological evaluations including H&E staining were conducted on the eye sections, while PAS staining was used to quantify the goblet cells in the conjunctiva.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The retention time on the ocular surface
ce:section.ce:para: The study employed a nano-enzyme hydrogel comprising PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel. Each drop was found to contain the same sodium fluorescein content. The eye drops were administered topically to the corneal surface. Four manual blinks were conducted at one-minute intervals. The course of the various substances, namely PBS, PEI-CD, OHAgel, and OHACDgel, was observed following the administration of eye drops, with the eye surface serving as the baseline. The observation periods were 0, 60, 120, 180, and 240 s. The ocular surface was observed with a slit lamp to obtain general images.

A statistical analysis of the data between groups was conducted using GraphPad Prism 9.0 software, employing the student's t-test. A p-value of less than 0.05 was deemed statistically significant for the comparison between groups.

Hailing Yu: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Xinxi Yu: Data curation. Yin Huang: Investigation. Ting Yu: Methodology. Huimin Lan: Project administration. Qianqian Zhang: Project administration. Yongquan Huang: Resources. Xin Peng: Resources, Project administration. Zebo Jiang: Writing – original draft, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Zhuhai Science and Technology Innovation Bureau (No.2420004000007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jconrel.2025.01.081.

Prog. Retin. Eye Res.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-atom nanozyme eye drops attenuate inflammation and break the vicious cycle in dry eye disease
Nanomicro Lett
